blurb: Tender tubers that explode into bold, geometric blooms from midsummer to frost.
seasons: Prolific color midsummer–first frost. TENDER: not winter-hardy in Boulder — dig & store tubers, replant after last frost (~mid-May)
wildlife: Open/single types feed bees; double 'dinnerplate' types offer little pollen
toxic: Mildly toxic to pets if eaten

care.hardiness: Tender — hardy only to USDA zones 7–10; not winter-hardy on the Boulder Front Range (zone 5b–6a). Tubers will freeze and rot if left in the ground through a Colorado winter. Treat dahlias as summer performers: plant in spring after the last frost, enjoy them through fall, then dig and store tubers before hard freeze. Done correctly, a single set of tubers can provide years of blooms.[1][2]
care.sun: Full sun — at least 6–8 hours of direct sun daily. On the Front Range, the intense high-altitude sun is an asset; dahlias bloom heavily and color is vivid. Some light afternoon shade is tolerated in extreme heat but generally reduces flower count.[1][2]
care.soil: Organically rich, medium-moisture, well-drained soil. Amend with compost before planting. Good drainage is non-negotiable — waterlogged soil rots tubers both in the ground and in storage. Front Range clay-loam benefits from compost incorporation to improve drainage and fertility.[2]
care.water: Moderate and consistent — dahlias are not xeric. Water deeply once or twice a week once plants are actively growing; do not irrigate between planting and sprouting (the tubers rot easily in cold, wet soil before shoots emerge). In the semi-arid Front Range climate, regular supplemental irrigation is essential from June through early October.[1][2]
care.depth: Plant tubers horizontally, flat on their side, 6 in deep for taller varieties (over ~3 ft); 2–3 in deep for compact bedding types. Set the eye (growth bud) facing up. Do not water immediately after planting — wait until shoots appear above soil, then begin regular irrigation.[1]
care.spacing: Space 18–24 in apart for most garden dahlias; allow up to 36 in for large 'dinnerplate' types. Shelter from strong winds — tall varieties need staking at planting time. Pinching growing tips when plants are 12–15 in tall encourages branching and more flowers.[1][2]
care.bloom: Mid-July through the first hard frost, typically early October on the Front Range — one of the longest bloom windows of any garden flower. Open and single-flowered types are prime pollinator plants through this period.[2]
care.feeding: Feed monthly with a low-nitrogen fertilizer (e.g. 5-10-10 or 0-20-20); excess nitrogen drives leafy growth at the expense of flowers. Work compost into the soil before planting for a slow, steady nutrient base. Container-grown dahlias need more frequent feeding through the summer.[2]
care.maintenance: Stake taller varieties at planting — once they topple in wind they're hard to recover. Deadhead regularly to extend bloom. After the first hard frost blackens the foliage (typically early October), cut stems to 4–6 in, carefully dig the clump with a fork, and shake off loose soil. Let clumps air-dry upside-down for a day or two, then divide or store whole. Pack in barely damp vermiculite, peat, or dry sand in a ventilated, frost-free location at 40–45°F (40–50°F is the safe range — below 40°F risks the tubers freezing solid if the space dips to 32°F or below). Check periodically through winter — if tubers shrivel, mist lightly; if they rot, remove affected ones. Replant the following spring after the last frost (~mid-May around Boulder).[1][2]
care.troubles: Aphids, caterpillars, spider mites, and leafhoppers are the main pest pressures in Colorado; check undersides of leaves and treat with insecticidal soap early. Powdery mildew appears in late summer when nights cool and humidity rises — improve airflow and avoid overhead watering. Crown gall, root rot, and viral diseases (mosaic) occur occasionally; buy certified virus-free tubers and remove any with mottled or distorted foliage. Deer and rabbits browse dahlias readily — fencing or repellent is worth considering.[2]
care.companions: Zinnias, cosmos, snapdragons, and salvias share dahlias' summer-to-frost bloom window and full-sun requirements. Annual grasses add texture behind dinnerplate types. Pair with bold foliage plants (canna, elephant ear) for a tropical border feel, or with bronze fennel and cleome for a looser cottage look.[2]
care.planting: Plant tubers in the ground only after all danger of frost is past — around mid-May in Boulder. Earlier planting in cold, wet soil rots tubers before they sprout; wait for soil to reach at least 60°F. Dahlias also do well in containers (minimum 12×12 in for most varieties; go bigger for dinnerplate types): start tubers in pots indoors a couple of weeks before the last frost to get a jump on the season, then move outside after mid-May. Container-grown dahlias dry out fast in summer — check soil daily and water more often than in-ground plants. In fall, containers offer no extra protection: pot roots are just as cold-exposed as ground roots, and the tubers will freeze solid as temperatures drop into the teens and below. Bring pots to a frost-free garage before the first hard freeze (typically early October), or dig the tubers out and store them exactly as you would in-ground ones (40–45°F, barely damp medium, dark and ventilated). Containers must never be left outside over a Boulder winter.[1][2]
care.propagation: The easiest and most reliable method is tuber division: when you dig the clump in fall (or just before replanting in spring), use a clean, sharp knife to cut the clump so that each division has at least one eye (growth bud) attached to a neck of the mother stem — eyes are easiest to see in spring when they start to swell. Discard any rotten or shriveled sections. A clump that started as one tuber may yield 5–15 divisions after a good season. Basal stem cuttings taken in late winter from tubers started indoors (cut 3–4 in shoots with a heel when they are 4–6 in tall, root in moist perlite at 65–70°F with bottom heat) produce many virus-free plants and are the method nurseries use to scale up named varieties. Growing from seed is straightforward for species and some open-pollinated mixes (start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost at 70°F; seedlings bloom their first year), but cultivar offspring vary wildly and named varieties don't come true — use seed only if you're happy with a surprise.[1][2]

edible.summary: Dahlia tubers were historically a food (eaten by the Aztecs) and the petals are an edible garnish — but flavor and quality are highly variable, the plant is toxic to pets, and the sap can irritate skin, so it is a caution-level edible rather than a reliable vegetable.[3]
edible.parts: Two parts have a food history: the tubers, which the Aztecs cultivated and ate and which can be cooked (texture compared to water chestnut or jicama, flavor ranging from sweet to bitter and inconsistent between cultivars), and the petals, which are non-toxic to people and used fresh as a colorful garnish on salads and desserts. The leaves and stems are not eaten and are toxic to pets.[3]
edible.preparation: Eat tubers cooked, and only tubers you have grown yourself organically — retail/ornamental tubers are routinely treated with chemicals not intended for consumption. Expect variable, sometimes bitter flavor; sample a small amount of a given cultivar first. Use petals raw as a garnish, picking clean, unsprayed blooms. Wear gloves when digging or dividing and wash skin afterward.[3]
edible.caution: Dahlia is listed by the ASPCA as toxic to dogs, cats, and horses — eating the tubers, stems, or leaves causes mild gastrointestinal upset and mild dermatitis in animals — so keep tubers and trimmings away from pets. For people, the plant contains polyacetylene compounds that can cause skin irritation, and phototoxic dermatitis (a sun-triggered skin reaction) is possible after handling the sap, so wear gloves. Anyone with daisy-family (Asteraceae) allergies may also react. Treat retail tubers as ornamental only — never eat tubers that may have been chemically treated, and don't assume every cultivar tastes good.[3]
